title: Programmable Persistent Random Walks in Active Brownian Particles Govern Emergent Dynamics
authors: Tarun Sunkesula Raghavendra, Yogesh Shelke, Stijn van der Ham, Anpuj Nair S, Hanumantha Rao Vutukuri
abstract: Self-propelled particles serve as minimal models for emulating the dynamic self-organization of microorganisms, yet most synthetic systems remain limited to a single mode of motion, namely active Brownian particles (ABPs). Here, we present an experimental strategy to encode various persistent random walks in ABPs by combining light-modulated propulsion strength with magnetic control of propulsion direction. Our system enables programmable Levy walks with tunable step-length distributions, run-and-tumble dynamics, self-avoiding random walks, and Gaussian walks, with on-demand switching between motion modes within a single experiment. In addition, particles are steered along complex trajectories such as Fibonacci spirals and nested polygons. Beyond single-particle behavior, we show that propulsion modes influence clustering dynamics by comparing ABPs with chiral active particles undergoing circular motion. These results establish a versatile platform for investigating how encoded motion at the level of individual particles governs transport, search strategies, and emergent organization in active matter systems.
summary: This paper presents a new experimental method to program the motion of active Brownian particles using light and magnetism. By controlling these forces, the researchers can switch between different types of random walks, such as Levy walks and run-and-tumble dynamics, within a single experiment. They also demonstrate the ability to steer particles along complex geometric paths. Finally, they show that the specific type of individual particle motion significantly affects how the particles cluster together, providing a tool to study emergent organization in active matter.
main_problem: Most synthetic active matter systems are limited to a single mode of motion, making it difficult to study how different types of individual particle dynamics affect collective behavior.
main_result: The authors developed an experimental strategy to program various persistent random walks (Levy, run-and-tumble, self-avoiding, and Gaussian) in active Brownian particles using light and magnetic fields, demonstrating that these motion modes directly influence clustering and emergent organization.
method: The researchers combined light-modulated propulsion strength with magnetic control of propulsion direction to encode specific trajectories and motion modes in synthetic active particles.
model_or_system: The system consists of active Brownian particles (ABPs) that can be manipulated via light and magnetic fields to emulate different types of self-propelled motion, including chiral active particles.
key_observables: Step-length distributions, particle trajectories (e.g., Fibonacci spirals, nested polygons), and clustering dynamics.
important_parameters: Propulsion strength, propulsion direction, and the specific type of random walk (Levy, run-and-tumble, etc.).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitation of current ABP systems, presents the experimental strategy for programmable motion, details the implementation of various random walk types and complex trajectories, and concludes by analyzing the impact of these modes on collective clustering dynamics.
why_interesting: This work is relevant to researchers in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics and open quantum systems as it provides a platform to study how programmable microscopic stochasticity and broken detailed balance drive emergent many-body organization and transport.
